perturbation.original_text: All obligations of Receiving Party with respect to any Conf idential Information received from Disclosing Party under the terms of this Agreement shall s urvive and continue for five (5) years after the date of the last discl osure of Confidential Informati on by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All obligations of Receiving Party with respect to any Confidential Information received from Disclosing Party under the terms of this Agreement shall survive and continue for one (1) year after the date of the last disclosure of Confidential Information by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies a five-year confidentiality period. Shortening this to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law, particularly if the information is highly sensitive and rapidly loses value.
perturbation.law_explanation: While New York doesn't have a codified trade secrets act like the UTSA, the courts recognize trade secret protection. A one-year confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient to protect a company's trade secrets, especially when considering the nature of the information and the industry standards, thereby failing to provide adequate protection under the law, as interpreted by New York courts. CPLR 3101 governs discovery, including trade secrets, implying a right to protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a five-year confidentiality period, which was shortened to one year. The perturbation explanation highlights that this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under New York law. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that a one-year period may be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the nature of the information and industry standards. Although the scraped snippets are not useful here, the inconsistency in the confidentiality period creates a contradiction as it potentially fails to provide adequate protection for trade secrets as recognized by New York courts.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees that it shall not, and shall cause it Representative not to, remove, overprint or deface any notice of confidentiality, copyright, t rademark, logo, legend or other notices of ownership or confidentiality from any original or copies of the Confidential Information it obtains from Disclosing Party, or authorize any third party to do any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees that it shall, and shall cause it Representative to, remove, overprint or deface any notice of confidentiality, copyright, trademark, logo, legend or other notices of ownership or confidentiality from any original or copies of the Confidential Information it obtains from Disclosing Party, and authorize any third party to do any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: The original text prohibits removing or defacing copyright notices. The modified text mandates the removal of copyright and trademark notices, potentially leading to copyright and trademark infringement.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 1202 prohibits intentionally removing or altering copyright management information. The modified text directly contradicts this by requiring the Receiving Party to remove copyright notices, potentially leading to violations of the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ). (4) Definitions.—For purposes of this subsection: (A) An “analog video cassette recorder” means a device that records, or a device that includes a function that records, on electromagnetic tape in an analog format the electronic impulses produced by the video and audio portions of a television program, motion picture, or other form of audiovisual work. (B) An “analog video cassette camcorder” means an analog video cassette recorder that contains a recording function that operates through a camera lens and through a video input that may be connected with a television or other video playback device. (C) An analog video cassette recorder “conforms” to the automatic gain control copy control technology if it— (i) detects one or more of the elements of such technology and does not record the motion picture or transmission protected by such technology; or (ii) records a signal that, when played back, exhibits a meaningfully distorted or degraded display. (D) The term “professional analog video cassette recorder” means an analog video cassette recorder that is designed, manufactured, marketed, and intended for use by a person who regularly employs such a device for a lawful business or industrial use, including making, performing, displaying, distributing, or transmitting copies of motion pictures on a commercial scale. (E) The terms “VHS format,” “8mm format,” “Beta format,” “automatic gain control copy control technology,” “colorstripe copy control technology,” “four-line version of the colorstripe copy control technology,” and “NTSC” have the meanings that are commonly understood in the consumer electronics and motion picture industries as of the date of the enactment of this chapter. (5) Violations.—Any violation of paragraph (1) of this subsection shall be treated as a violation of subsection (b)(1) of this section. Any violation of paragraph (2) of this subsection shall be deemed an “act of circumvention” for the purposes of section 1203(c)(3)(A) of this chapter. 1202. Integrity of copyright management information 3 (a) False Copyright Management Information.—No person shall knowingly and with the intent to induce, enable, facilitate, or conceal infringement— (1) provide copyright management information that is false, or (2) distribute or import for distribution copyright management information that is false. (b) Removal or Alteration of Copyright Management Information.— No person shall, without the authority of the copyright owner or the law— (1) intentionally remove or alter any copyright management information, (2) distribute or import for distribution copyright management information knowing that the copyright management information has been removed or altered without authority of the copyright owner or the law, or (3) distribute, import for distribution, or publicly perform works, copies of works, or phonorecords, knowing that copyright management information has been removed or altered without authority of the copyright owner or the law, knowing, or, with respect to civil remedies under section 1203, having reasonable grounds to know, that it will induce, enable, facilitate, or conceal an infringement of any right under this title. (c) Definition.—As used in this section, the term “copyright management information” means any of the following information conveyed in connection with copies or phonorecords of a work or performances or displays of a work, including in digital form, except that such term does not include any personally identifying information about a user of a work or of a copy, phonorecord, performance, or display of a work: (1) The title and other information identifying the work, including the information set forth on a notice of copyright. (2) The name of, and other identifying information about, the author of a work. (3) The name of, and other identifying information about, the copyright owner of the work, including the information set forth in a notice of copyright. (4) With the exception of public perfo

perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates the removal of copyright notices, directly contradicting 17 U.S.C. § 1202, which prohibits intentionally removing or altering copyright management information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in subsection (b), "Removal or Alteration of Copyright Management Information.— No person shall, without the authority of the copyright owner or the law— (1) intentionally remove or alter any copyright management information". The modified contract requires the Receiving Party to do exactly what this law prohibits, creating a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable l aw, agrees that in the event a party brings any action, suit or proceedings under, or seeks en forcement of any provision pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation any action for a tempor ary or permanent injunction (a) to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal and state courts o f the party against whom the action or enforcement is sought , (b) to irrevocably waive any objection to the laying of the venue of any such proceeding brought in such a court and any claim that any such proceeding brought in such a court has been brought in an inconvenient or inappropriate forum, and (c) to irrevocably waive any right to a trial by jury in any such proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that in the event a party brings any action, suit or proceedings under this Agreement (a) to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Cohoes, NY, (b) to irrevocably waive any objection to the laying of the venue of any such proceeding brought in such a court and any claim that any such proceeding brought in such a court has been brought in an inconvenient or inappropriate forum, and (c) to irrevocably waive any right to a trial by jury in any such proceeding, irrespective of the nature of the claim or amount in controversy.
perturbation.explanation: While parties can generally agree to arbitration, the unconscionable waiver of a jury trial irrespective of the claim violates fundamental rights, particularly if there's a power imbalance. Also, restricting jurisdiction solely to the courts of Cohoes, NY may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 7th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution and New York State Constitution, Article I, § 2
perturbation.law_explanation: The Seventh Amendment to the U.S. Constitution guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases exceeding a certain value. The blanket waiver of a jury trial, regardless of the claim, could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, especially if one party has significantly less bargaining power. Article I, Section 2 of the NY Constitution similarly guarantees the right to a jury trial.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by restricting jurisdiction solely to the courts of Cohoes, NY, and by including a blanket waiver of a jury trial irrespective of the claim. This contradicts the Seventh Amendment, which preserves the right to a jury trial in civil cases exceeding twenty dollars. The modified text removes this right. The scraped snippet from the U.S. Constitution states that 'the right of trial by jury shall be preserved.' The perturbation explanation also notes the unconscionable waiver of a jury trial irrespective of the claim violates fundamental rights.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Receiving Party may issue a statement that contains Confidentia l Information if, in the opinion of its legal counsel, it is required to do so by applicable laws and if it a dvises Disclosing Party, at least forty-eight hours in advance, of such pending disclosure and consults with Disclosing Party as to the contents of the statement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Receiving Party may issue a statement that contains Confidential Information if required to do so by applicable laws; notification to Disclosing Party is not required.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify the Disclosing Party at least 48 hours in advance of disclosing confidential information, when legally compelled, infringes on the Disclosing Party's right to seek protective measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26(c) allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information during discovery. By removing the notification requirement, the Receiving Party prevents the Disclosing Party from seeking such a protective order, thereby hindering their ability to safeguard confidential information as permitted by law. While this isn't a direct violation, it obstructs the Disclosing Party's ability to utilize legal mechanisms designed to protect confidential information in legal proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Receiving Party to notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing confidential information when legally compelled. This directly impacts the Disclosing Party's ability to seek protective measures under FRCP 26(c), as explained by the Law Explanation. While the snippets provided do not directly mention FRCP 26(c) or notification requirements, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, along with the type Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction, state that removing the notification requirement prevents the Disclosing Party from utilizing legal mechanisms to protect confidential information, which implies a contradiction of legal principles. The absence of notification directly hinders the Disclosing Party from safeguarding their confidential information as permitted by law.
